title: Victoire Pisa

infobox.value: (Japanese: ヴィクトワールピサ) is a retired Japanese [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") racehorse and active [sire](/wiki/Sire_(horse) "Sire (horse)"). After winning three of his four starts as a juvenile, the colt won the [Satsuki Shō](/wiki/Satsuki_Sh%C5%8D "Satsuki Shō") at [Nakayama Racecourse](/wiki/Nakayama_Racecourse "Nakayama Racecourse") in April 2010. After an unsuccessful campaign in Europe he returned to Japan to win the [weight-for-age](/wiki/Weight-for-age "Weight-for-age") invitational [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen") in December. In the following spring he was sent to [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai") where he raced for the first time on a [synthetic track](/wiki/Synthetic_racetrack_surfaces_for_horse_racing "Synthetic racetrack surfaces for horse racing") in the [Dubai World Cup](/wiki/Dubai_World_Cup "Dubai World Cup"). He defeated an international field to become the first Japanese-trained horse to win the world's most valuable race. His subsequent career was restricted by injury problems and he was retired at the end of 2011.

**Victoire Pisa** (Japanese: ヴィクトワールピサ) is a retired Japanese [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred "Thoroughbred") racehorse and active [sire](/wiki/Sire_(horse) "Sire (horse)"). After winning three of his four starts as a juvenile, the colt won the [Satsuki Shō](/wiki/Satsuki_Sh%C5%8D "Satsuki Shō") at [Nakayama Racecourse](/wiki/Nakayama_Racecourse "Nakayama Racecourse") in April 2010. After an unsuccessful campaign in Europe he returned to Japan to win the [weight-for-age](/wiki/Weight-for-age "Weight-for-age") invitational [Arima Kinen](/wiki/Arima_Kinen "Arima Kinen") in December. In the following spring he was sent to [Dubai](/wiki/Dubai "Dubai") where he raced for the first time on a [synthetic track](/wiki/Synthetic_racetrack_surfaces_for_horse_racing "Synthetic racetrack surfaces for horse racing") in the [Dubai World Cup](/wiki/Dubai_World_Cup "Dubai World Cup"). He defeated an international field to become the first Japanese-trained horse to win the world's most valuable race. His subsequent career was restricted by injury problems and he was retired at the end of 2011.

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

Victoire Pisa is a dark bay horse bred at the [Shadai Farm](/wiki/Shadai_Stallion_Station "Shadai Stallion Station") in [Hokkaido](/wiki/Hokkaido "Hokkaido"). His sire [Neo Universe](/wiki/Neo_Universe "Neo Universe"), a son of the thirteen-time [leading sire in Japan](/wiki/Leading_sire_in_Japan "Leading sire in Japan") [Sunday Silence](/wiki/Sunday_Silence "Sunday Silence"), was a successful Japanese performer, winning the Satsuki Shō and the [Tokyo Yūshun](/wiki/Tokyo_Y%C5%ABshun "Tokyo Yūshun"), the first two legs of the [Japanese Triple Crown](/wiki/Triple_Crown_of_Thoroughbred_Racing#Japan "Triple Crown of Thoroughbred Racing") in 2003. At stud he has sired the Satsuki Shō winner Unrivaled and the Tokyo Yūshun winner Logi Universe.[[4]](#cite_note-4) Victoire Pisa's dam Whitewater Affair was a successful British racehorse who won the [John Porter Stakes](/wiki/John_Porter_Stakes "John Porter Stakes") and the [Prix de Pomone](/wiki/Prix_de_Pomone "Prix de Pomone") as a four-year-old in 1997.[[5]](#cite_note-5) At stud in Britain she bred the [Yasuda Kinen](/wiki/Yasuda_Kinen "Yasuda Kinen") winner Asakusa Denen, and was then exported to Japan where her other foals included the Group Three winner Swift Current. The colt was prepared for racing by [Katsuhiko Sumii](/wiki/Katsuhiko_Sumii "Katsuhiko Sumii"), the trainer of [Delta Blues](/wiki/Delta_Blues_(horse) "Delta Blues (horse)") and [Vodka](/wiki/Vodka_(horse) "Vodka (horse)").

## Racing career

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Racing career")]

### 2009: two-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: 2009: two-year-old season")]

Victoire Pisa opened his racing career with three races at [Kyoto Racecourse](/wiki/Kyoto_Racecourse "Kyoto Racecourse") in late 2009. On 25 October he ran in a nine furlong event for unraced horses in which he finished second to [Rose Kingdom](/wiki/Rose_Kingdom "Rose Kingdom"), a colt who went on to win the [Japan Cup](/wiki/Japan_Cup "Japan Cup"). Two weeks later he recorded his first win in a ten furlong maiden race. On 28 November Victoire Pisa started the [1/2](/wiki/Fractional_odds "Fractional odds") favourite for the [Kyoto Nisai Stakes](/wiki/Kyoto_Nisai_Stakes "Kyoto Nisai Stakes") and won by one and a half [lengths](/wiki/Horse_length "Horse length"). On his final start of the year he was moved up in class for the domestic Group Three [Radio Nikkei Hai Nisai Stakes](/wiki/Hopeful_Stakes_(Japan) "Hopeful Stakes (Japan)") at [Hanshin Racecourse](/wiki/Hanshin_Racecourse "Hanshin Racecourse") on 26 December. Starting the 3/5 favourite he took the lead near the finish and won by a neck from Cosmo Phantom.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### 2010: three-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: 2010: three-year-old season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4a/Victoire-Pisa20100418%282%29.jpg/250px-Victoire-Pisa20100418%282%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoire-Pisa20100418(2).jpg)

Victoire Pisa after winning the Satsuki Shō

Victoire Pisa began his three-year-old season in March at [Nakayama Racecourse](/wiki/Nakayama_Racecourse "Nakayama Racecourse") in the Hochi Hai Yayoi Shō, a trial race for the Satsuki Shō. Despite having to be switched to obtain a clear run he took the lead 50 yards from the line and won by half a length from Eishin Apollon. In the Satsuki Shō a month later he started the 1.3/1 favourite in a field of eighteen three-year-old colts. Ridden by [Yasunari Iwata](/wiki/Yasunari_Iwata "Yasunari Iwata") he again struggled to get a clear run in the straight before accelerating through a gap along the rail to take the lead inside the final furlong and win from [Hiruno d'Amour](/w/index.php?title=Hiruno_d%27Amour&action=edit&redlink=1 "Hiruno d'Amour (page does not exist)"), [Eishin Flash](/wiki/Eishin_Flash "Eishin Flash") and Rose Kingdom.[[7]](#cite_note-7) On 30 May Victoire Pisa started 11/10 favourite for the [Tokyo Yushun](/wiki/T%C5%8Dky%C5%8D_Y%C5%ABshun "Tōkyō Yūshun") over one and a half miles at [Tokyo Racecourse](/wiki/Tokyo_Racecourse "Tokyo Racecourse"). Yet again, he had a troubled passage, and was in eighth place two furlongs from the finish. Despite making steady progress in the closing stages he finished third behind Eishin Flash and Rose Kingdom.

Victoire Pisa was then sent to Europe where he was trained with the [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe "Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe") as his main objective. He finished fourth behind [Behkabad](/wiki/Behkabad "Behkabad") in the [Prix Niel](/wiki/Prix_Niel "Prix Niel") at [Longchamp Racecourse](/wiki/Longchamp_Racecourse "Longchamp Racecourse") in September and then finished seventh of the nineteen runners behind [Workforce](/wiki/Workforce_(horse) "Workforce (horse)") in the Arc a month later.[[8]](#cite_note-8) On his return from Europe, Victoire Pisa was made a 27/1 outsider for the [Japan Cup](/wiki/Japan_Cup "Japan Cup") at Tokyo on 28 November. In a controversial race the colt took the lead in the straight but was overtaken by [Buena Vista](/wiki/Buena_Vista_(horse) "Buena Vista (horse)") inside the final furlong and lost second place on the line to Rose Kingdom. An inquiry by the racecourse stewards saw the winner disqualified for causing interference to the runner-up.[[9]](#cite_note-9) Victoire Pisa was one of fifteen horses invited to contest the Arima Kinen on 26 December at Nakayama. Ridden for the first time by the Italian [jockey](/wiki/Jockey "Jockey") [Mirco Demuro](/wiki/Mirco_Demuro "Mirco Demuro") he started at odds of 7.4/1 in a field which included Buena Vista, Eishin Flash, [Dream Journey](/wiki/Dream_Journey "Dream Journey") and Oken Bruce Lee. In front of a crowd of 120,813 Victoire Pisa was always among the leaders and moved up quickly to take the lead entering the straight.[[10]](#cite_note-bloodhorse1-10) He held off the late challenge of the favourite Buena Vista to win by a nose.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Demuro described the winner as "a fantastic horse" with a "great heart".[[10]](#cite_note-bloodhorse1-10)

At the end of the season, Victoire Pisa was voted Japanese Champion Three-year-old colt.[[2]](#cite_note-japanracing1-2)

### 2011: four-year-old season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: 2011: four-year-old season")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Victoire-Pisa20120115.jpg/250px-Victoire-Pisa20120115.jpg)](/wiki/File:Victoire-Pisa20120115.jpg)

Victoire Pisa at his retirement ceremony in January 2012

On his first appearance as a four-year-old, Victoire Pisa started the 2/5 favourite for the Nakayama Kinen over nine furlongs on 27 February. He took the lead in the straight and won comfortably by two and a half lengths from Captain Thule. The colt was then sent to the [United Arab Emirates](/wiki/United_Arab_Emirates "United Arab Emirates") to contest the [Dubai World Cup](/wiki/Dubai_World_Cup "Dubai World Cup") at [Meydan Racecourse](/wiki/Meydan_Racecourse "Meydan Racecourse") on 26 March. The race was run over ten furlongs on a synthetic [Tapeta footings](/wiki/Synthetic_racetrack_surfaces_for_horse_racing#Tapeta "Synthetic racetrack surfaces for horse racing") surface and attracted runners from Britain, Ireland, South Africa, Saudi Arabia and the United States. Demuro restrained the colt in last place before moving up to join the leaders half a mile from the finish of a slowly run race. He took the lead in the straight and held off the challenge of his fellow Japanese challenger Transcend by half a length with [Monterosso](/wiki/Monterosso_(horse) "Monterosso (horse)") in third and [Cape Blanco](/wiki/Cape_Blanco_(horse) "Cape Blanco (horse)") in fourth.[[12]](#cite_note-12) Victoire Pisa became the first Japanese-trained horse to win the "world's richest race".[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-BBC9437297-14) The victory was an emotional one for the Japanese contingent, coming less than three weeks after the country had been struck by an [earthquake and tsunami](/wiki/2011_T%C5%8Dhoku_earthquake_and_tsunami "2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami"): Demuro returned to the winner's enclosure in tears, whilst Katsuhiko Sumii said that he felt that "the whole country was behind me."[[14]](#cite_note-BBC9437297-14) Victoire Pisa was sent to Europe again but did not contest any races after sustaining a series of leg injuries.[[15]](#cite_note-racingpost1-15)

Victoire Pisa returned to Japan at the end of the year but was unable to reproduce his early season form. He finished thirteenth behind Buena Vista in the Japan Cup and eighth behind [Orfevre](/wiki/Orfevre "Orfevre") in the Arima Kinen. He was retired from racing at the end of December.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Sumii commented on the colt's retirement by saying that "I feel I should apologise to him because I couldn't get him back to his best but at least I am happy that I can send him to the stud without any further accidents".[[15]](#cite_note-racingpost1-15) Victoire Pisa was named [Japanese Champion Older Male](/wiki/JRA_Award "JRA Award") for 2011.[[3]](#cite_note-japanracing2-3)

## Stud record

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Stud record")]

Victoire Pisa was retired from racing to become a breeding stallion at the [Shadai Stallion Station](/wiki/Shadai_Stallion_Station "Shadai Stallion Station"). He made an immediate impact by siring the [Oka Sho](/wiki/Oka_Sho "Oka Sho") winner [Jeweler](/wiki/Jeweler_(horse) "Jeweler (horse)") in his first crop of foals. [Panja Tower](/wiki/Panja_Tower "Panja Tower") won the [NHK Mile Cup](/wiki/NHK_Mile_Cup "NHK Mile Cup") with Victoire Pisa as the broodmare sire.

In December 2017, Victoire Pisa was moved from Shadai Stallion Station to the Breeders Stallion Station,[[20]](#cite_note-20) where he stood stud for three seasons before being sold to Turkey along with Kluger, another Japanese-bred stallion.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Victoire_Pisa&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

An [anthropomorphized](/wiki/Moe_anthropomorphism "Moe anthropomorphism") version of Victoire Pisa appears in the *[Umamusume: Pretty Derby](/wiki/Umamusume:_Pretty_Derby "Umamusume: Pretty Derby")* franchise, voiced by [Hana Shimano](/wiki/Hana_Shimano "Hana Shimano").[[23]](#cite_note-23)
